title: Jonah
author: Unknown; Jonah son of Amittai is the protagonist (see 2 Kings 14:25)
date: ~760 BC (if Jonah wrote it); possibly later
setting: The Assyrian capital Nineveh — Israel's feared enemy — to which Jonah is called to preach. The book is unusual among the prophets in being entirely narrative.
purpose: To reveal the breadth of God's compassion — extending to Gentile enemies — and to challenge Jewish exclusivism. Jonah is a mirror of Israel's own reluctance to be a light to the nations.
themes: God's compassion for all nations, even enemies
The folly and futility of fleeing from God
Repentance and divine relenting from judgment
Jonah as a type of Christ (Matthew 12:40)
The Ninevites' repentance contrasted with Israel's hardness

outline.label: Jonah Flees; the Storm and the Fish
Jonah's Prayer from the Fish's Belly

before.label: Elijah flees to Horeb — the despairing prophet God pursues
Jeroboam II expands Israel's territory — Jonah's other prophecy
Nineveh: capital of Assyria, Israel's most feared enemy

after.label: Nahum — Nineveh's eventual permanent destruction prophesied
The sign of Jonah — Jesus' death and resurrection
Acts — the gospel proclaimed to the nations as God intended

key_people.name: Jonah ben Amittai
key_people.role: Israelite prophet who attempts to flee from God's call, is swallowed and vomited by a great fish, and finally preaches to Nineveh — with the largest per-capita revival in biblical history, and profound personal resentment

key_people.name: The Sailors
key_people.role: Pagan sailors who, through their encounter with Jonah's God, come to 'fear the LORD exceedingly' (1:16) — the first of several gentiles in the book who respond better to YHWH than Jonah himself does

key_people.name: The King of Nineveh
key_people.role: The Assyrian king who orders a city-wide fast when Jonah's message arrives; his response is presented as a model of repentance — more thorough than anything Israel's own kings had managed

context: Jonah ben Amittai is mentioned in 2 Kings 14:25 as a prophet during Jeroboam II's reign (c. 793–753 BC), and the book is set during a period when Assyria was temporarily weakened and before Nineveh became the fully dominant empire. Nineveh was the capital of Assyria, Israel's most feared enemy. The book's theological point depends on this historical context: Jonah's resistance to the mission is theologically motivated — he does not want God to spare Israel's enemy.
christ_connection: Jesus explicitly cites the sign of Jonah as the only sign he will give his generation: 'just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matt 12:40). Jonah's death-and-return from the fish is the OT type of which Jesus' burial and resurrection is the antitype. Jesus also contrasts the Ninevites' repentance at Jonah's preaching with his own generation's refusal to repent at the presence of 'something greater than Jonah' (Matt 12:41).

key_verses.note: 'Salvation belongs to the LORD' — Jonah's prayer from the fish; the theological thesis of the book, which Jonah knows intellectually but must learn experientially
God saw their works, that they turned from their evil way, and God relented — the Ninevites' repentance is genuine and God's response is real; judgment is not inevitable once repentance comes

Jonah's complaint reveals his theology: 'I knew that you are a gracious God and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster' — Jonah knows who God is; he just wishes God weren't that way

themes_detail.title: The Inescapable Sovereignty of God
themes_detail.text: Every element in Jonah's flight — the storm, the fish, the vine, the worm, the east wind — is sent directly by God. The book presents a God whose sovereignty over creation means that no direction is away from him. Jonah cannot flee from the presence of the LORD not because God is omnipresent in the abstract but because God actively pursues him through every element of the created order. The theology is not a philosophical argument for omnipresence but a narrative demonstration that YHWH is the God of the sea as well as the land, the God of pagan sailors as well as Israelite prophets.

themes_detail.title: God's Compassion for the Nations
themes_detail.text: The book ends with God's rhetorical question that remains unanswered: 'Should I not pity Nineveh, that great city, in which there are more than 120,000 persons who do not know their right hand from their left?' (4:11). This ending is deliberately provocative: the reader is left to answer the question, and the only possible answer is 'yes, of course you should.' Jonah's resentment at God's mercy exposes the self-serving nationalism that the covenant was never meant to produce. God's compassion for pagan Nineveh is not an anomaly but a revelation of his character.

themes_detail.title: Repentance and Its Consequences
themes_detail.text: The Ninevites' repentance is the most thoroughgoing in the OT: from the king on his throne to the animals in the field, the entire city fasts and puts on sackcloth. God's response is immediate — he 'relented of the disaster that he had said he would do to them.' This is theologically significant: God's threatened judgments are genuinely conditional on human response (Jer 18:7–10 makes the principle explicit). Jonah, who knows God's character (4:2), resents this conditionality when it applies to Israel's enemies. His resentment is the book's central theological target.

themes_detail.title: The Education of a Prophet
themes_detail.text: Jonah's character arc is not triumphant: at the end of the book he is sitting outside Nineveh, sulking about the withered vine, still preferring his own comfort and his ethnic prejudices to God's universal mercy. The book does not record his repentance or conversion — it leaves the reader suspended with God's question. This literary choice is the book's most powerful device: the reader's identification with Jonah makes the question personal. Do we want God's mercy extended to people we regard as enemies? The book refuses a comfortable answer.
